results.possibleIdentity: Albert Kung was an engineer and physicist associated with the IBM Watson Laboratory (Watson Scientific Computing Laboratory) at Columbia University in New York City during the late 1960s. He conducted research under the auspices of the laboratory as a graduate student, receiving his PhD from Columbia University. His doctoral dissertation, titled "Nuclear Magnetic Resonance in Type II Superconductors," was completed under the sponsorship of the prominent physicist Alfred G. Redfield (A.G. Redfield). His expertise spanned the fields of electrical engineering and physics. In the 1980s, an engineer by the same name, Dr. Albert Kung, was active as a professional at Grumman and was involved with the IEEE Long Island Section, where he lectured on the emerging technology of personal computers. His profile aligns with the participants of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which frequently collaborated with technical specialists from major industrial laboratories like IBM in New York during that era.
